retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Indications -- Crown Exposure
retrieved_docs.content: Altered passive eruption ("gummy smile") with bone approximating or covering the cementoenamel junction, resulting in an excess gingival display; no restoration is needed

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Complications -- Crown Lengthening Surgeries
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth that have undergone crown lengthening surgeries will appear longer than before, and the interdental papilla will no longer reach the original interproximal contact. Therefore, restorations must account for these changes by having longer interproximal contacts. Teeth undergoing crown lengthening surgery must also be provisionalized for at least 6 weeks after crown lengthening. Patients must be instructed on how to perform oral hygiene for provisional restorations and prevent accidental fracture or dislodgment of provisional restorations. Since crown lengthening removes crestal bone and available bone height to vital structures, in rare cases, crown lengthening may preclude future implant therapy as there may no longer be enough bone for the placement of implants. In the anterior maxilla, crown lengthening of teeth on one side may require matching crown lengthening on the other side to achieve an esthetic result.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The complications that can manifest with drug-induced gingival overgrowth are as follows: Functional difficulties Esthetic concerns Increased incidence of caries Delayed eruption of teeth Pathological drifting of teeth Prolonged retention of the primary dentition Diastemas and spacing problems Poor plaque control leads to periodontal complications [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- History and Physical -- Clinical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical findings of DIGO include the following: Firm, painless, nodular enlargement of the interdental papilla, limited to the keratinized portions of the gingiva and extending to the facial and lingual gingival margins. In severe cases, a large fold of hypertrophied gingival tissue is observed covering the crowns. If no secondary inflammation is present, it appears firm and pale pink with minute lobulations, pouting from underneath the gingival margin. This is delineated by a groove of tissue that does not bleed on touch. If secondary inflammation exists, the gingiva appears smooth and red or bluish-red. The enlargement is generalized but usually greater in the anterior regions. Typically, it is not seen in edentulous areas. [15] The gingival overgrowth disappears when teeth are extracted. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Indications -- Wedge Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Residual pocketing at a distal or mesial tooth surface that faces excessively thick gingival tissue Typically at the most distal tooth of the arch, adjacent to thick tissue of the maxillary tuberosity or the retromolar pad

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Complications -- Pocket Reduction Surgeries
retrieved_docs.content: Complications specific to periodontal surgery include the possibility that the surgery may not achieve the desired outcome and the unique risks associated with each type of periodontal surgery. Pocket depth reduction surgeries typically result in some degree of gingival recession, leading to a longer appearance of the teeth and exposed root surfaces.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of periodontal surgery can be categorized into two groups: those common to any oral surgical procedure and those specific to periodontal surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Indications -- Gingivectomy
retrieved_docs.content: Altered passive eruption ("gummy smile") with excess gingiva covering tooth but crestal bone remaining 2 mm apical to the cementoenamel junction Removal of genetic or medication-induced gingival enlargement Removal of pigmented gingiva (racial pigmentation tends to return long-term)

retrieved_docs.title: Excessive Gingival Display -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Around 10% of the population between 20 to 30 years of age presents excessive gingival display, which is more prevalent in women. [9] [10] The prevalence of the condition decreases with age due to the dropping of the upper and lower lips, which reduces the exposure of the gingiva and maxillary incisors. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Complications -- Pocket Reduction Surgeries
retrieved_docs.content: Gingival recession may also prevent the complete filling of interdental spaces, resulting in unattractive open spaces known as black triangles. In cases of free gingival grafting surgery, the grafted areas may have a different color, leading to poor gingival esthetics. After the gingival recession, exposed root surfaces are also more susceptible to root caries, especially without effective oral hygiene practices. Pocket reduction surgery, particularly osseous surgery involving removing supporting bone in teeth already affected by significant bone loss, may increase tooth mobility. However, tooth mobility often improves during the healing process, as the gingival attachment, which contributes significantly to tooth support in severe periodontitis, is reestablished during periodontal surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Crown lengthening may not be possible if restorative margins extend into furcations or result in excessive bone removal. Crown exposure is not indicated in excessive gingival display caused by hyperactive lip muscles, skeletal maxillary excess, or a short upper lip.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance -- Natal and Neonatal Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of natal or neonatal teeth can potentially cause ulceration on the ventral surface of the tongue, often due to the sharp incisal edge of the tooth. [24] This condition is referred to as Riga-Fede disease or syndrome. Since the root development of these teeth is incomplete, they are mobile, increasing the risk of swallowing and aspiration teeth. Other potential complications include injury to the mother's breast and difficulties in the infant's ability to suckle. [24] The mobility of these teeth has also been linked to the degeneration of Hertwig epithelial root sheath, which plays a crucial role in root formation. The deterioration of this sheath further contributes to incomplete root development.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: For periodontal pocket reduction surgery, several local factors may reduce chances of regeneration, attachment gain, and pocket reduction, including root proximity, tipping or rotation of teeth, tooth mobility, thin mucosal tissue, the orientation of furcation entrances, enamel pearls, cervical enamel projections, denticles, ridges, root groves, and impacted teeth.

retrieved_docs.content: Some authors define a gummy smile as more than 3 to 4 mm of exposed gingival tissue in a smile, whereas others consider more than 2 mm of gingival exposure as excessive. [13] In general, a gum-to-lip distance of 4 mm or more during a smile is deemed "unattractive" by dentists. [14] Once we identify that a patient has excessive gingival exposure, the next step is to determine its etiology (Table 1). Table 1. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to loss of teeth caused by an accident, indicating that dental injury resulting from an accident can lead to unintended physical contact.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1989
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids for local oral treatment and other agents for local oral treatment in ATC are both classified as stomatological preparations, indicating that each is a type of medication formulated for use within the oral cavity to manage dental or mucosal conditions.
